g.data-narration: Welcome to learning NAND and NOR universal gates. These special gates can implement any Boolean function, revolutionizing digital circuit design.
g.text: Universal Gates in Digital Logic Design

g.data-narration: Universal gates are special because just one type can build ANY digital circuit. NAND and NOR are the two most important universal gates.
g.text: NAND and NOR are called universal gates because any Boolean function
can be implemented using ONLY NAND gates OR ONLY NOR gates.

g.data-narration: Using only one gate type simplifies chip manufacturing, reduces costs, and increases circuit density. This is why NAND is preferred in modern CMOS technology.
To create a NOT gate using NAND, simply connect both inputs together. When A equals A, NAND produces A' (inversion).
g.text: NAND as Universal Gate - NOT Implementation

g.data-narration: To create AND using NAND, use two NAND gates. The first performs NAND operation, the second inverts the result to get AND.
g.text: NAND as Universal Gate - AND Implementation

g.data-narration: To create OR using NAND, invert both inputs first (using NAND as inverters), then NAND the results. This applies De Morgan's theorem.
g.text: NAND as Universal Gate - OR Implementation

g.data-narration: Similar to NAND, connect both inputs of a NOR gate together. NOR with A on both inputs produces A' (NOT).
g.text: NOR as Universal Gate - NOT Implementation

g.data-narration: To create AND using NOR, first invert both inputs with NOR gates, then NOR the results. This implements AND via De Morgan's theorem.
g.text: NOR as Universal Gate - AND Implementation

g.data-narration: To create OR using NOR, use two NOR gates. First performs NOR, second inverts the result to produce OR output.
g.text: NOR as Universal Gate - OR Implementation

g.data-narration: Two-level NAND-NAND implementation converts SOP expressions. Replace AND gates with NAND, then replace OR with NAND gate.
g.text: Any SOP expression can be converted to NAND-NAND:

g.data-narration: Example: F = AB + CD converts to NAND-NAND by replacing each AND with NAND, and the OR with NAND, showing functional equivalence.
Two-level NOR-NOR implements POS expressions. Replace OR gates with NOR, and AND gate with NOR to get equivalent logic.
g.text: Any POS expression can be converted to NOR-NOR:

g.data-narration: Example: F = (A+B)(C+D) converts to NOR-NOR by replacing OR gates with NOR and AND with NOR for equivalent POS implementation.
When SOP has a single literal like C in F = AB + C, we need extra inverter because NAND expects complemented inputs.
g.text: When SOP has a single literal as product term, it must be inverted before
connecting to the second-level NAND gate.

g.data-narration: NAND is faster in CMOS technology (PMOS in parallel), uses common IC 7400. NOR is slower but essential for POS implementations.
Key takeaways: NAND and NOR are universal gates. SOP converts to NAND-NAND, POS to NOR-NOR. Single variables need extra inverters.
g.text: ✓ NAND and NOR are universal gates - each can implement any Boolean function alone
✓ SOP → NAND-NAND: Replace AND gates and OR gate with NAND gates
✓ POS → NOR-NOR: Replace OR gates and AND gate with NOR gates

g.data-narration: These concepts enable VLSI chip fabrication, FPGAs, and efficient CPU design. NAND gates form the foundation of modern digital systems.
g.text: NAND gates are the primary building block in modern digital systems
